data.title: Franklin Covey widens 2025 guidance to $265M–$275M amid cost reductions and market uncertainty
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Franklin Covey Co. (FC) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Paul Walker highlighted that "despite an external environment that continues to be uncertain and where, as a result of this uncertainty, organizations are scrutinizing costs at a greater level and delaying many investment decisions," third quarter revenue was $67.1 million, with adjusted EBITDA of $7.3 million, which Walker noted "was higher than the top end of our expected range of between $4 million and $6.5 million."
  • Walker stated the company completed a series of cost reductions designed "to offset the impact of these factors on our profitability," expecting these actions "will help offset declines in revenue related to government actions and to the general uncertainty in the economy."
  • The CEO reported strong traction from the go-to-market transformation in the Enterprise North America business, noting "we're really encouraged by the traction and the early results we're achieving" and described the restructuring of the sales force into dedicated teams for new and existing clients as complete.
  • Walker cited a notable new logo win with a $6 billion information management company, resulting in "a $2.3 million contract with about $1.7 million scheduled to deliver in the coming quarters."
  • The CEO also emphasized robust client retention and a "15-point increase in the number of clients who expanded outside of their renewal period compared to the third quarter last year."
  • Walker announced the appointment of Dariusz Paczuski as Chief Marketing Officer on June 1 to lead expanded marketing efforts.
  • In Education, Walker stated "we still expect to grow our Education business year-over-year on both the top and bottom lines and expect our addition of new schools and our school retention results to be as good, if not better this year than they were last year."
  • CFO Jessica Betjemann said, "Total revenue in the third quarter was $67.1 million and finished in line with our previously shared expectation, at the low end," and that "gross margin for the third quarter remained strong and was approximately flat year-over-year at 76.5% of revenue."
  • Betjemann explained, "We incurred $4.7 million in expenses for these restructuring activities, primarily for severance and related costs," and the cost actions "resulted in savings of $3 million in this quarter and results in $4 million savings in Q4 and an annualized run rate savings of $8 million in fiscal year 2026."

Outlook

  • The company revised its fiscal 2025 guidance, now expecting revenue in the range of $265 million to $275 million.
  • Adjusted EBITDA guidance widened to $28 million to $33 million, with CFO Betjemann noting, "the midpoint and top end of this range is consistent with our previous guidance due to the cost reduction actions we took in the third quarter that will produce savings to beneficially impact this year."
  • Management explained the revision reflects "continued uncertainty impacting our clients' decision-making" and "the timing risk for the delivery of services for some contracts we have closed in the last 90 days and expect to close this quarter that could slip into the first quarter of the next fiscal year."
  • Updated guidance for fiscal year 2026 will be provided at year-end, but management expects "a meaningful increase in adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow in fiscal year 2026."

Financial Results

  • Third quarter revenue was $67.1 million, down 9% from the prior year quarter, but up 13% sequentially.
  • Adjusted EBITDA was $7.3 million, exceeding the top end of the prior guidance range.
  • Operating expenses rose to $53.5 million, primarily due to a $4 million increase in restructuring charges and $1.6 million in higher SG&A tied to the new go-to-market initiative.
  • Free cash flow through the third quarter totaled $10.6 million compared to $30.6 million for the same period last year, with year-to-date cash flow from operating activities at $19 million.
  • At quarter end, liquidity was over $95 million, with $33.7 million in cash and no drawdowns on the $62.5 million credit facility.
  • During the quarter, the company repurchased approximately 372,000 shares for $8.3 million, with $27.9 million remaining under the current buyback authorization.

Q&A

  • Alexander Paris, Barrington Research, asked about additional milestones in the Enterprise division and pipeline growth. Paul Walker explained the company is "continuing to see our pipelines grow," highlighted strong marketing activity, and noted a 60% services attach rate in the Enterprise division.
  • Paris also questioned expectations for new schools in Education. Walker responded, "we do expect that the way that business is pacing is that we'll be there or potentially even a little bit better" compared to the prior year.
  • Paris sought clarification on the $10 million guidance reduction by bucket; Walker indicated the primary delta relates to the timing of service delivery, especially in Education and Enterprise.
  • Jeff Martin, ROTH Capital, inquired about the environment for fiscal '26. Walker said, "we're certainly planning with that in mind, that tariffs and other actions...that uncertainty, hopefully, it will eventually go away. But...we're not planning that it will."
  • Nehal Chokshi, Northland Capital Markets, pressed on why subscription revenue invoiced was down 8% year-over-year despite strong new logo and expansion activity. Walker cited client downsizing, smaller expansion dollar amounts, and a large client converting to an intellectual property agreement as key factors.
  • Chokshi questioned the wide Q4 revenue range. Betjemann stated, "the delivery of services and the timing for decision-making...is tight. There is a possibility, though. So the top end of the range is reflecting that we were able to close and do everything in this quarter, but there's a realistic view that it may not happen and things could get pushed into next year."
  • David Storms, Stonegate, asked about Education materials revenue volatility. Walker explained materials revenue fluctuates based on the timing and size of district/statewide contracts.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts showed a slightly negative to neutral tone, focusing on guidance reductions, revenue timing uncertainty, and reconciling strong client metrics with falling subscription revenue. One analyst said, "given those 3 positive things, that would imply that invoice subscription revenue should be up, not down 8% year-over-year, even with these headwinds being cited."
  • Management maintained a confident but cautious tone, emphasizing cost actions, strategic initiatives, and future growth. Walker repeatedly stressed confidence: "we continue to be confident in the actions that we're undertaking to accelerate future growth."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management maintained a cautious approach, but management highlighted more concrete cost actions and a wider guidance range this quarter.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company revised annual revenue guidance down again, now to $265 million–$275 million, compared to $275 million–$285 million last quarter.
  • Adjusted EBITDA guidance widened to $28 million–$33 million, but the midpoint/top end remains in line with previous guidance due to increased cost savings.
  • Strategic focus shifted to cost reductions and maintaining investment in growth, while the previous quarter stressed the go-to-market transformation's early traction and expansion plans.
  • Analyst questions this quarter centered more on reconciling lower revenue with positive client activity, as well as the timing of revenue recognition and service delivery.
  • Management's tone remained cautiously optimistic, but with increased emphasis on prudent cost management and operational discipline.

Risks and Concerns

  • The uncertain macroeconomic environment and client cost scrutiny continue to impact sales cycles and service delivery timing, with management noting, "the uncertainty in the environment pushed some of these wins late enough into our year to create a timing risk."
  • Ongoing government actions, tariffs, and the end of ESSER funds present risks to both Enterprise and Education revenue.
  • Management cited the possibility of further timing-related revenue slippage into next fiscal year.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the wide revenue guidance range and the disconnect between client activity and subscription revenue trends.

Final Takeaway

Franklin Covey completed significant cost reductions and maintained strategic investment in growth initiatives, resulting in a widened annual guidance range of $265 million–$275 million revenue and $28 million–$33 million adjusted EBITDA. Management remains confident in the business model, citing strong client retention, expanding multiyear contracts, and early traction from go-to-market transformations, while acknowledging the impact of macroeconomic uncertainty and timing risks on near-term results. Looking ahead, the leadership expects actions taken this year to support a meaningful increase in adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow in fiscal 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: HSBC to sell UK life unit to Chesnara for $355M
data.description:
HSBC (NYSE:HSBC) agreed to sell its HSBC Life (UK) Limited unit to Chesnara Plc for £260M  (US$355M), Chesnara said in a filing on Thursday.

For HSBC (NYSE:HSBC), the deal continues a string of asset sales as the bank focuses more on Asia, amid a broader restructuring push to cut costs and boost profits. Last month, the bank agreed to sell its custody business in Germany. Last year, it agreed to sell its French life insurance business.

In contrast, Chesnara is consolidating life and pension books in the U.K. and the Netherlands, complemented by business written across the group's businesses in Sweden, the Netherlands, and the U.K., it said.

The deal is expected to generate lifetime cash generation of over £800M for Chesnara, through delivery of future profits and run off of capital requirements over time, the company said in the filing.

HSBC Holdings (HSBC) stock was unchanged in U.S. premarket trading on Thursday.

data.title: Salesforce's recent price increases are 'underappreciated' by investors: Morgan Stanley
data.description:
Salesforce (NYSE:CRM) recently announced price increases are “underappreciated” by investors, Morgan Stanley said on Thursday.

“While price increases targeted on Enterprise/Unlimited tiers represent modest tailwinds to FY27/FY28 [subscription revenue], we see strategic rationale inducing upgrades to Premium tiers as underappreciated,” analyst Keith Weiss wrote in a note to clients. “Pricing & Packaging remains a compelling catalyst for growth acceleration to re-rate shares.” 

Weiss, who has an Overweight rating and $404 price target on Salesforce, made similar comments last month. 

The price increases — which go into effect in August — were likely designed to push adoption of Salesforce's premium tiers and give customers more value for their bundles, Weiss said. 

“Using Sales Cloud as a proxy for potential cost savings of adopting higher tier bundles vs. adopting similar functionality on a standalone basis, we estimate Unlimited Edition to be ~70% more cost-effective when bundled, while the Agentforce 1 tier is a more material ~100%+ more cost-effective relative to Unlimited on a like-for-like functionality basis,” he posited. “As such, we expect [the]latest pricing action to catalyze upgrades to higher tiers for Salesforce's largest customers looking to maximize the value of their Salesforce solutions, particularly given Data Cloud/Agentforce functionality embedded within the Agentforce 1 tier.”

data.title: AST SpaceMobile secures additional $100M financing
data.description:
• AST SpaceMobile (NASDAQ:ASTS) on Thursday announced the closing of a $100M equipment financing facility led by Trinity Capital (NASDAQ:TRIN).
  • This non-dilutive financing is designed to support AST SpaceMobile’s accelerated manufacturing and network deployment goals during 2025 and 2026.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Krispy Kreme appoints Raphael Duvivier as CFO
data.description:
• Krispy Kreme (NASDAQ:DNUT) said on Thursday that Raphael Duvivier, President, International of Krispy Kreme, has been named Chief Financial Officer, effective July 11, 2025. 
  • Duvivier succeeds Jeremiah Ashukian, who decided to leave the company to pursue an opportunity with a private company.
  • Since joining Krispy Kreme in 2019, Duvivier has held multiple leadership roles at the company, including segment Chief Financial and Strategy Officer, International, and Chief Development Officer. 
  • He previously held various senior leadership positions in global finance, development, and operations at Restaurant Brands International (QSR). Prior to that, he held leadership positions at Opus Investimentos. 
  • Additionally, Chief Product Officer Alison Holder has been promoted to Chief Brand and Product Officer effective July 11, 2025, assuming responsibility for the company’s global marketing initiatives.

data.title: Alibaba plans to raise HK$12B via exchangeable bond offering
data.description:
Alibaba (NYSE:BABA) plans to raise about HK$12B ($1.53B) through an exchangeable bond offering to boost investments in cloud infrastructure and global commerce operations.

Alibaba proposed to issue about HK$12B aggregate principal amount of Zero Coupon Exchangeable Bonds due 2032 in a private offering to certain non-U.S. persons in offshore transactions outside the U.S.

The bonds link to Alibaba Health Technology, according to the group. Investors can later exchange these bonds for shares in Alibaba Health. The bonds will not bear regular interest.

Alibaba Group holds about 64% of equity interest in Alibaba Health.

Alibaba intends to use the net proceeds from the bond offering for general corporate purposes, including investments to support the development of its cloud infrastructure and international commerce businesses.

data.title: So-Young stock rises on regaining compliance with Nasdaq minimum bid price requirement
data.description:
• So-Young International (NASDAQ:SY) has regained compliance with the minimum bid price of $1.00 per share requirement set forth under the Nasdaq listing rule.
  • So-Young International (NASDAQ:SY) stock up 7% premarket.

data.title: CrowdStrike checks show 'very robust' demand as momentum continues: Wedbush
data.description:
Recent channel checks into CrowdStrike (NASDAQ:CRWD) showed demand for its cybersecurity products is “very robust” and that momentum is likely to continue into 2026, Wedbush Securities said.

“The company has seen strength driven by continued platform adoption with healthy momentum in Cloud, Identity, and Logscale coupled with better-than-expected trends in Data Protection and Charlotte AI, which saw considerable strength in deal flow in the most recent quarter, and we believe clearly accelerated this July quarter so far,” analysts at the firm wrote in a note to clients. “While AI continues to be top of mind for enterprises heading into an important year for AI monetization, we are seeing more module add-ons within CRWD’s core verticals as we are still in the very early innings of this trend playing out.”

Wedbush analysts raised their price target on CrowdStrike to $575 from $525 and reiterated their Outperform rating.

Delving deeper, the analysts said that CrowdStrike's revenue tied to cyber artificial intelligence products is “significantly” above where it was a year ago. That trend is likely to continue to accelerate, thanks to the strength and stickiness of its Falcon platform.

“CRWD remains the gold standard for cybersecurity with the company in the early innings of its multi-year growth story with [net new annual recurring revenue] growth expected to reaccelerate in the [second-half of 2026], and we believe that CRWD remains at the forefront of securing the AI Revolution over the coming years,” the analysts wrote.

data.title: Nippon Steel to raise $5.6B in subordinated loans to fund US Steel takeover
data.description:
Nippon Steel (OTCPK:NPSCY) (OTCPK:NISTF) said on Wednesday it will seek to raise ¥800B (~$5.6B) through two subordinated loans to partially fund its recent $14.9B acquisition of US Steel and refinance previous loans.

Nippon Steel (OTCPK:NPSCY) (OTCPK:NISTF) will use a ¥500B subordinated loan to partially repay a ¥2T bridge loan secured in June for the deal, and a separate ¥300B loan will refinance a previous ¥450B yen subordinated loan, according to Reuters.

The remaining 1.5T of the bridge loan will be financed through a combination of methods, based on an assessment of interest rates, market conditions and other factors, a company spokesperson said.

Following the US Steel deal, Nippon Steel's (OTCPK:NPSCY) (OTCPK:NISTF) debt-to-equity ratio rose to ~0.8 from 0.35 as of March 31 due to the bridge loans and a loss on the sale of its stake in a U.S. joint venture with ArcelorMittal, which it decided to sell to help win approval for the US Steel acquisition.

The company aims to reduce the debt-to-equity ratio to ~0.7 by the end of March 2026 through measures such as cash flow from earnings and asset sales, Reuters said.
